question: Does the HSPB1 gene have pathogenic variants documented in ClinVar for Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease?
ideal_answer: Yes, HSPB1 (heat shock protein family B (small) member 1, Gene ID 3315) has multiple pathogenic and likely pathogenic variants documented in ClinVar that are specifically linked to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 2F (CMT2F; MedGen C1847823), the HSPB1-associated axonal subtype of CMT. The exact count depends on the database snapshot; the RDF portal query returned 20 current records at time of query, while the live NCBI ClinVar release contains ≥66 such variants. Variants include missense substitutions affecting functionally critical residues in the alpha-crystallin domain (e.g., c.379C>T p.Arg127Trp, c.404C>T p.Ser135Phe, c.406C>T p.Arg136Trp, c.418C>G p.Arg140Gly, c.545C>T p.Pro182Leu), as well as frameshift and nonsense variants (e.g., c.109dup p.Arg37fs (Likely pathogenic), c.532G>T p.Glu178Ter (Likely pathogenic)). Several large deletions at the HSPB1 locus on chromosome 7q11.23 also appear in the dataset. CMT2F is an autosomal dominant axonal peripheral sensorimotor neuropathy characterised by distal muscle weakness, sensory loss, and reduced tendon reflexes with onset ranging from the first to sixth decade.
baseline_answer: Yes, the HSPB1 gene has pathogenic variants documented in ClinVar for Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease. HSPB1 (heat shock protein family B member 1) encodes a small heat shock protein, and mutations in this gene cause Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 2F (CMT2F) and distal hereditary motor neuropathy type 2B (dHMN2B). ClinVar contains multiple pathogenic and likely pathogenic variants in HSPB1 associated with these conditions, which are axonal forms of inherited peripheral neuropathy. These variants are typically missense mutations that affect the protein's function in maintaining neuronal integrity and axonal transport. The disease inheritance pattern is autosomal dominant, and affected individuals present with progressive distal muscle weakness and atrophy, primarily in the lower limbs, along with sensory loss in CMT2F cases.
togomcp_answer: Yes. The HSPB1 gene has extensive pathogenic variant documentation in ClinVar for Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, with 342 variant records associated with this condition. Multiple missense variants are classified as pathogenic or likely pathogenic, including c.404C>T (p.Ser135Phe), c.379C>T (p.Arg127Trp), c.545C>T (p.Pro182Leu), c.406C>T (p.Arg136Trp), c.418C>G (p.Arg140Gly), and c.295C>A (p.Leu99Met). These variants are primarily linked to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease axonal type 2F (CMT2F; OMIM 606595) and the related distal hereditary motor neuronopathy type 2B (dHMN2B; OMIM 608634), both conditions affecting the peripheral nervous system. Several of these pathogenic variants have been reviewed by multiple submitters with no conflicts in their pathogenic classification, providing strong clinical evidence for their disease association.

question: How many reviewed human proteins are annotated with chemotaxis-related GO biological process terms?
ideal_answer: There are 326 reviewed human proteins annotated with chemotaxis-related GO biological process terms. These proteins span 70 distinct chemotaxis-related GO terms, including the parent term "chemotaxis" (GO:0006935) and 69 descendant terms covering cell-type-specific chemotaxis (such as leukocyte, neutrophil, macrophage, and lymphocyte chemotaxis), directional responses (positive and negative chemotaxis, chemoattraction and chemorepulsion), and specialized forms like axon guidance chemotaxis and energy taxis. The annotations encompass proteins involved in immune cell recruitment, neural development, and microbial motility. Major functional categories include chemokine receptors, integrins, and signaling molecules that regulate directed cell migration in response to chemical gradients.

These proteins include chemokines such as CCL21, CCL22, CCL24, and CCL25, chemokine receptors, cytokines like tumor necrosis factor ligand superfamily member 14, complement components, and diverse signaling molecules involved in directing cell migration in response to chemical gradients.

question: How many reviewed human proteins annotated with at least one autophagy biological process term (GO:0006914 or its descendants) have at least one approved catalytic reaction?
ideal_answer: Among the 284 reviewed human proteins annotated with autophagy-related biological process terms (GO:0006914 and its 25 descendant terms spanning macroautophagy, microautophagy, mitophagy, pexophagy, reticulophagy, and other selective autophagy subtypes), 65 have at least one approved catalytic reaction. These 65 proteins represent approximately 23% of human autophagy proteins and encompass diverse enzyme classes: serine/threonine kinases including the AMPK alpha subunits (PRKAA1, PRKAA2) that phosphorylate autophagy regulators; cysteine proteases such as ATG4A and ATG4B that cleave LC3 family members from autophagosomal membranes; ATPases including the polyamine transporter ATP13A2 that moves spermine and spermidine across lysosomal membranes; phospholipid scramblases ATG2A and ATG2B that transfer lipids to expanding phagophores; and tyrosine kinases such as ABL1 that phosphorylate autophagy substrates. All reactions linked to these proteins are chemically balanced and approved, confirming that their enzymatic mechanisms are precisely documented at the atomic level.

question: Summarize the distribution of melatonin biosynthesis enzymes across species and characterize their molecular functions and the chemical intermediates involved.
ideal_answer: Melatonin biosynthesis enzymes are distributed across 16 species spanning three kingdoms (Animalia, Plantae, and Fungi), with 33 reviewed proteins in total. Mammals account for 14 proteins across 9 taxa, including two human paralogs (AANAT and ASMT), with coverage extending to mouse, rat, cattle, sheep, macaque, hamster, chimpanzee, and Mus musculus molossinus. Plants contribute 14 proteins from 4 taxa: Arabidopsis thaliana (4 proteins), Oryza sativa subsp. japonica (7 proteins, the most of any single organism), Oryza sativa subsp. indica (1 protein), and Zea mays (2 proteins). Additional representatives include two bird proteins in Gallus gallus, two insect proteins in Drosophila melanogaster, and one fungal protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae. The pathway proceeds through two enzymatic steps acting on distinct chemical intermediates: first, aralkylamine N-acetyltransferase (AANAT, GO:0004059, a subclass of N-acetyltransferase activity) converts serotonin (CHEBI:28790, C10H12N2O) to N-acetylserotonin (CHEBI:17697, C12H14N2O2); then acetylserotonin O-methyltransferase (ASMT, GO:0017096, a subclass of S-adenosylmethionine-dependent methyltransferase activity) converts N-acetylserotonin to melatonin (CHEBI:16796, C13H16N2O2). Exactly 17 proteins are annotated with AANAT activity and 16 with ASMT activity, with no overlap. This enzymatic conservation across evolutionarily distant taxa — from yeast to plants to mammals — indicates an ancient origin of the two-step melatonin biosynthesis mechanism, with the pathway starting from tryptophan (CHEBI:27897, C11H12N2O2) via serotonin as the upstream precursor.
baseline_answer: Melatonin biosynthesis enzymes are distributed across diverse taxonomic groups, including vertebrates, invertebrates, plants, fungi, and bacteria, though the enzymatic pathways show significant variation. In vertebrates, melatonin synthesis occurs primarily in the pineal gland and retina through a well-characterized four-step pathway beginning with the amino acid L-tryptophan. Tryptophan hydroxylase (TPH) catalyzes the conversion of tryptophan to 5-hydroxytryptophan, which aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (AADC) then converts to serotonin (5-hydroxytryptamine). The rate-limiting enzyme arylalkylamine N-acetyltransferase (AANAT) acetylates serotonin to form N-acetylserotonin, and finally, hydroxyindole-O-methyltransferase (HIOMT, also called ASMT) methylates this intermediate to produce melatonin (N-acetyl-5-methoxytryptamine). In non-vertebrate animals such as insects and crustaceans, AANAT homologs exist but HIOMT appears absent or divergent, suggesting alternative terminal steps. Plants utilize a parallel but distinct pathway where tryptophan decarboxylase produces tryptamine, which is then hydroxylated to serotonin by tryptamine 5-hydroxylase; plant SNAT (serotonin N-acetyltransferase) and caffeic acid O-methyltransferase (COMT)-like enzymes complete the synthesis. In photosynthetic organisms, melatonin synthesis occurs in chloroplasts and mitochondria. Bacteria and fungi possess melatonin but the enzymatic machinery remains poorly characterized, with evidence suggesting convergent evolution of biosynthetic capabilities. AANAT exhibits the greatest sequence diversity across species, reflecting its role as the regulatory enzyme controlling melatonin rhythm, while the chemical scaffold of indole derivatives remains conserved throughout evolution.
togomcp_answer: Melatonin biosynthesis involves four key enzymes distributed across eukaryotes, with notable presence in both animals and plants. The pathway begins with tryptophan 5-hydroxylase (EC 1.14.16.4), which catalyzes the rate-limiting hydroxylation of L-tryptophan to 5-hydroxy-L-tryptophan using tetrahydrobiopterin as a cofactor; this enzyme is found in 14 reviewed organisms, predominantly vertebrates (11 species) and mammals (9 species), existing as two isoforms (TPH1 and TPH2) in mammals. The second enzyme, aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (EC 4.1.1.28), decarboxylates 5-hydroxy-L-tryptophan to serotonin and is present in 14 organisms spanning 11 metazoan and 3 plant species, displaying broad substrate specificity for aromatic amino acids. Serotonin N-acetyltransferase (EC 2.3.1.87), the penultimate enzyme, acetylates serotonin to N-acetylserotonin using acetyl-CoA and shows the widest distribution with 17 organisms including 11 animals and 5 plants; in vertebrates, this enzyme controls the circadian rhythm of melatonin production in the pineal gland, while plant homologs localize to chloroplasts and participate in stress responses. The final enzyme, acetylserotonin O-methyltransferase (EC 2.1.1.4), methylates N-acetylserotonin to melatonin using S-adenosyl-L-methionine and occurs in 14 organisms evenly distributed between animals (7 species including 6 mammals) and flowering plants (7 species); plant versions often possess dual functionality, acting on both melatonin precursors and phenylpropanoid compounds. The chemical intermediates—L-tryptophan, 5-hydroxy-L-tryptophan, serotonin, N-acetylserotonin, and melatonin—form a conserved biosynthetic sequence, though an alternative minor pathway exists in which 5-methoxytryptamine is directly acetylated to melatonin, suggesting evolutionary plasticity in the terminal methylation and acetylation steps across different organisms.

question:
Among the following kinase groups — AGC, CAMK, CMGC, and TK — which has the greatest number of human protein targets with experimentally determined X-ray crystal structures deposited in the PDB?

Choose one of the following options:
  - AGC
  - CAMK
  - CMGC
  - TK
ideal_answer:
TK (tyrosine kinases) has the greatest number of human protein targets with
PDB X-ray crystal structures, with 2,280 structures across 80 ChEMBL-classified
human targets. CMGC ranks second (1,539 structures, 58 targets), followed by
CAMK (734 structures, 65 targets) and AGC (422 structures, 59 targets).

TK dominates structural coverage because tyrosine kinases are the primary targets
in cancer drug discovery. The EGFR family alone (EGFR, HER2, HER3, HER4) accounts
for hundreds of entries; BCR-ABL1 (P00519) has over 200 structures due to
imatinib-resistance studies; VEGFR2/KDR (P35968), ALK (Q9UM73), c-MET (P08581),
RET (P07949), FGFR1-4, and the SRC family kinases each contribute dozens to
hundreds of structures. The pharmaceutical industry's intense focus on TK
inhibitors for oncology has driven an outsized crystallographic effort relative
to the number of targets.

CMGC ranks second because it includes intensively studied targets: CDK2 (P24941)
alone has over 400 PDB structures from fragment-based and structure-based drug
design campaigns; CDK4/6 (cyclin-dependent kinases), CDK8, CDK9, MAPK1/ERK2
(P28482), MAPK14/p38α (Q16539), GSK3B (P49841), and DYRK1A (Q13627) are all
major drug targets with large structural datasets.

CAMK ranks third despite having the most targets (65) among the four groups,
because many CAMK members (e.g., CaM kinases, DAPK, CHK1, CHK2) are less
pharmacologically tractable or have been structurally solved fewer times.

AGC ranks last (422 structures) despite its prominent members (AKT1/2/3, PKCα,
PKA, RSK1-4, S6K1) because several AGC kinases proved challenging for structural
biology due to flexibility or membrane association, and the group has fewer
members overall.

A note on cross-group overlap: 70 PDB structures (1.4%) contain proteins with
dual classification in ChEMBL (6 proteins annotated as both AGC and CAMK). These
structures are counted in both groups' totals. This overlap does not affect the
answer — TK leads CMGC by 741 structures, far exceeding the 70-structure overlap.
baseline_answer:
**TK** is correct.

The tyrosine kinase (TK) group has the greatest number of human protein targets with experimentally determined X-ray crystal structures in the PDB among these four kinase groups. This predominance reflects both the historical focus on tyrosine kinases in drug discovery efforts, particularly for cancer therapeutics, and their clinical importance as therapeutic targets. Receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) and non-receptor tyrosine kinases have been extensively studied structurally due to their roles in oncogenic signaling pathways, leading to numerous crystal structures of kinases like EGFR, ABL, SRC, and many others in complex with various inhibitors and in different conformational states. While the AGC, CAMK, and CMGC groups also contain important kinases with solved structures, the sheer number of TK structures—driven by pharmaceutical industry interest in developing kinase inhibitors for cancer and other diseases—significantly exceeds the other groups in terms of deposited PDB entries.
togomcp_answer:
**TK (Tyrosine kinase)**

The TK group has 78 human proteins with experimentally determined X-ray crystal structures deposited in the PDB, significantly more than the other kinase groups. In comparison, CAMK has 46 proteins with X-ray structures, CMGC has 43, and AGC has 41. Tyrosine kinases represent the largest kinase family in the human kinome with 90 total reviewed proteins in UniProt, and their critical roles in cell signaling, cancer biology, and as drug targets have driven extensive structural characterization efforts, resulting in the highest structural coverage (87%) among the four groups.

question: How many human metalloprotease drug targets with experimental 3D structures are documented in ChEMBL?
ideal_answer: There are 69 human metalloprotease drug targets with experimental 3D structures documented in ChEMBL. These were identified by integrating three databases: UniProt proteins annotated with metallopeptidase activity (GO:0008237 and all 12 descendant GO terms, confirmed via OLS4:getDescendants), ChEMBL, and PDB. From 185 human metalloproteases (reviewed SwissProt entries), 92 are registered as ChEMBL single-protein drug targets, and 91 have at least one experimental PDB structure cross-referenced in UniProt. The intersection — proteins that are both ChEMBL drug targets and structurally characterized — is 69, representing 75% structural coverage of the metalloprotease drug target space. This high coverage reflects the pharmaceutical importance of metalloproteases, particularly matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), ADAMs, and aminopeptidases, which are extensively pursued for cancer, arthritis, and cardiovascular disease. The integration of ChEMBL bioactivity data with PDB structural data across the complete metalloprotease family reveals that the vast majority of drug-relevant metalloproteases have been structurally characterized, providing a foundation for structure-based drug design.
baseline_answer: I cannot provide a specific numerical answer to this question as it requires querying the ChEMBL database directly, which I do not have access to. ChEMBL is a manually curated database of bioactive molecules with drug-like properties, and it contains information about drug targets, including human metalloproteases with experimental 3D structures. The exact count would depend on the current version of ChEMBL and would require filtering the database for human proteins classified as metalloproteases that have both documented drug activity and experimental structural data (typically from the Protein Data Bank). This number changes as new structures are deposited and new drug-target relationships are curated. To obtain an accurate count, one would need to query ChEMBL using its web interface or API with appropriate filters for taxonomic classification (human), protein class (metalloprotease), and availability of experimental 3D structures.
togomcp_answer: ChEMBL documents 21 human metalloprotease drug targets with experimental 3D structures in the Protein Data Bank. These comprise 11 matrix metalloproteases (MMPs 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, 13, 14, 16, and 20), eight ADAM/ADAMTS family proteases (ADAM8, ADAM10, ADAM17, ADAM33, ADAMTS1, ADAMTS4, ADAMTS5, and ADAMTS13), and two angiotensin-converting enzymes (ACE and ACE2). ACE2 has the most structures with 331 entries, reflecting intensive structural characterization driven by its role as the SARS-CoV-2 receptor, while ACE has 85 structures. Among the MMPs, collagenase 3 (MMP-13) leads with 49 structures, followed by stromelysin-1 (MMP-3) with 44 structures. ADAM17, a key sheddase involved in inflammatory signaling, has 31 structures. The remaining targets range from 1 to 29 structures each, with 13 of the 34 total human metalloprotease drug targets in ChEMBL lacking any experimental structural data.

question: Does the SPG11 gene, a causative gene for hereditary spastic paraplegia type 11, satisfy all three of the following conditions: (1) a MANE Select transcript in Ensembl, (2) an experimentally-determined protein structure in PDB, and (3) a drug target entry in ChEMBL?
ideal_answer: Yes, the SPG11 gene (ENSG00000104133) associated with hereditary spastic paraplegia type 11 satisfies all three conditions. (1) MANE Select transcript: ENST00000261866 in Ensembl carries the MANE Select quality flag (ENSGLOSSARY_0000365), indicating it is the highest-quality representative isoform chosen through collaboration between NCBI and Ensembl. (2) PDB structures: the encoded protein Spatacsin (UniProt: Q96JI7, mnemonic SPTCS_HUMAN, Swiss-Prot reviewed) has three cryo-EM structures deposited: 8YAD (SPG11-SPG15 complex), 8YAB (AP5 complex bound to SPG11-SPG15), and 8YAH (full-length AP5 complex bound to SPG11-SPG15), all determined by electron microscopy single-particle reconstruction. (3) ChEMBL drug target: Spatacsin is registered as a SINGLE PROTEIN target in ChEMBL (CHEMBL6067204), linked to Q96JI7 via a TargetComponent/skos:exactMatch cross-reference, with at least one binding assay (CHEMBL5652514) on record.
baseline_answer: The SPG11 gene does not satisfy all three specified conditions. While SPG11 has a MANE Select transcript in Ensembl (ENST00000393480.8, corresponding to the canonical isoform of spatacsin, the protein encoded by SPG11), it lacks both an experimentally-determined protein structure in the Protein Data Bank and a drug target entry in ChEMBL. Spatacsin is a large protein (approximately 2,400 amino acids) involved in lysosomal and autophagic processes, and mutations in SPG11 represent the most common cause of autosomal recessive hereditary spastic paraplegia with thin corpus callosum (SPG11). Despite its clinical significance, the full-length structure of spatacsin has not been experimentally resolved by X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy, or cryo-electron microscopy, though computational models may exist. Additionally, SPG11/spatacsin is not catalogued as a therapeutic target in ChEMBL, as current treatment for SPG11-related disease remains symptomatic rather than targeted at the molecular level, and no small molecules or biologics have been developed to specifically modulate spatacsin function.
togomcp_answer: Yes, the SPG11 gene satisfies all three conditions. The gene (ENSG00000104133) has a MANE Select transcript (ENST00000261866) registered in Ensembl, confirming it as a high-quality reference transcript for clinical applications. The encoded protein spatacsin (UniProt Q96JI7) has three experimentally-determined structures deposited in the Protein Data Bank—entries 8YAB, 8YAD, and 8YAH, all solved by electron microscopy. Additionally, spatacsin is cataloged in ChEMBL as target CHEMBL6067204, establishing its presence in the bioactive compound and drug discovery database. The convergence of these three annotations indicates SPG11 has both high-quality genomic annotation and structural characterization, and is recognized as a potential therapeutic target despite hereditary spastic paraplegia type 11 being a rare genetic disorder.

question: Summarize the taxonomic distribution, oxygen tolerance characteristics, and typical culture conditions for anaerobic bacteria documented in bacterial diversity databases.
ideal_answer: The BacDive database documents 8,994 unique anaerobic strains (with 316 strains annotated under more than one category, yielding 9,310 total category assignments): 4,776 annotated as "anaerobe," 4,310 as "facultative anaerobe," and 224 as "obligate anaerobe." Across all three categories, Firmicutes is the dominant phylum, contributing 2,096 anaerobes, 1,686 facultative anaerobes, and 94 obligate anaerobes; Proteobacteria ranks second among anaerobes (810) and ties with Firmicutes among facultative anaerobes (1,683); Actinobacteria accounts for 676 anaerobes and 490 facultative anaerobes, while Bacteroidetes contributes 351 anaerobes and 111 facultative anaerobes. At the order level, Lactobacillales leads with 1,557 strains, followed by Clostridiales (861), Enterobacterales (741), Actinomycetales (557), and Eubacteriales (409). Temperature data from BacDive's structured CultureTemperature records show that general anaerobes (4,707 strains with temperature data) grow across an extraordinarily broad range of −2 to 103°C with an average optimum near 36.4°C, facultative anaerobes (4,224 strains) span 0–90°C with a cooler mean of 26.8°C reflecting their greater ecological breadth, and obligate anaerobes (216 strains) average 36.9°C with a range of −1.8 to 112°C indicating adaptation to extreme environments. Cross-database integration with MediaDive identifies 4,984 anaerobic strains with linked medium records; the most commonly used media are Columbia Blood Medium (744 strains), EG Medium (402), Trypticase Soy Yeast Extract Medium (357), PYG Medium modified (330), and BL Agar (249), reflecting the nutritionally rich, reduced-oxygen formulations required by these organisms.
baseline_answer: Anaerobic bacteria are taxonomically diverse and distributed across multiple phyla, with major representatives in Firmicutes (including Clostridium, Peptostreptococcus, and Eubacterium), Bacteroidetes (Bacteroides, Prevotella), Actinobacteria (Propionibacterium, Bifidobacterium), Fusobacteria, and Proteobacteria (Desulfovibrio). These organisms exhibit varying oxygen tolerance levels: obligate anaerobes are killed by atmospheric oxygen concentrations and require <0.5% O₂ for growth; aerotolerant anaerobes can survive oxygen exposure but do not use it metabolically; and facultative anaerobes can switch between aerobic and anaerobic metabolism depending on oxygen availability. Culture conditions for anaerobic bacteria typically require oxygen-free environments maintained through specialized anaerobic chambers, jars with gas-generating sachets (producing H₂ and CO₂ to scavenge oxygen), or roll-tube techniques using pre-reduced media. Media formulations include reducing agents such as cysteine, thioglycollate, or dithiothreitol to maintain low redox potentials (Eh -150 to -400 mV), along with resazurin or methylene blue as redox indicators. Optimal growth temperatures vary by species but commonly range from 30-37°C for mesophilic strains, while pH requirements typically fall between 6.5-7.5. Enriched media containing hemin, vitamin K, yeast extract, and volatile fatty acids support fastidious anaerobes, particularly those from clinical or gut microbiome sources. Incubation periods often extend 48-72 hours or longer due to slower growth rates compared to aerobic bacteria.
togomcp_answer: Anaerobic bacteria documented in BacDive exhibit broad taxonomic diversity spanning 35 phyla, with the greatest representation in Firmicutes (3,692 strains), Proteobacteria (2,492 strains), Actinobacteria (1,142 strains), Bacteroidetes (467 strains), and the archaeal phylum Euryarchaeota (361 strains). At the class level, Bacilli, Gammaproteobacteria, and Clostridia dominate, while obligate anaerobes are concentrated in genera such as Clostridium, Bacteroides, and Peptoniphilus. Oxygen tolerance characteristics segregate into three principal categories: general anaerobes (4,776 strains), facultative anaerobes (4,310 strains), and obligate anaerobes (224 strains), with facultative types demonstrating the broadest environmental tolerance. Culture temperature requirements vary substantially by oxygen tolerance phenotype, with obligate anaerobes typically cultivated at 37-41°C (range: -1.8 to 112°C), general anaerobes at 36-39°C, and facultative anaerobes at the cooler range of 27-32°C (range: 0 to 90°C), reflecting adaptation from psychrophiles to hyperthermophiles. pH optima cluster narrowly around neutral conditions (6.4-7.7), though documented tolerance spans pH 0.5 to 13.0 across the full diversity. Standard cultivation employs complex enriched media, with CIP Medium 72 (1,332 strains), CIP Medium 3 (949 strains), Columbia Blood Medium (726 strains), and specialized formulations including chopped meat medium, MRS medium for lactobacilli, and Postgate medium for sulfate-reducers representing the most commonly documented growth substrates.

question: Which human enzymes with catalytic roles in the leukotriene and eoxin biosynthesis pathway are documented drug targets in ChEMBL with mechanism-of-action records?
ideal_answer: Five of the eight human enzymes with catalytic roles in the Reactome leukotriene and eoxin biosynthesis pathway have mechanism-of-action records in ChEMBL. Arachidonate 5-lipoxygenase (ALOX5, CHEMBL215) has the most ChEMBL mechanism records of any pathway enzyme, with eight drugs documented as inhibitors, including zileuton (phase 4), the only FDA-approved drug specifically indicated for asthma that acts by reversibly inhibiting this rate-limiting step in leukotriene synthesis, as well as five other phase-4 anti-inflammatory agents (mesalamine, sulfasalazine, balsalazide disodium, olsalazine sodium, meclofenamate sodium) and two phase-2 clinical candidates (PF-4191834, atreleuton). The 5-lipoxygenase-activating protein (ALOX5AP/FLAP, CHEMBL4550) is the most clinically active target, with three distinct phase-3 clinical inhibitors — fiboflapon, fiboflapon sodium, and veliflapon — all documented as INHIBITOR mechanisms. Leukotriene A4 hydrolase (LTA4H, CHEMBL4618), which converts LTA4 to the potent chemoattractant LTB4, is targeted by acebilustat at phase 2. The multidrug resistance-associated protein ABCC1/MRP1 (CHEMBL3004), which exports LTC4 across the cell membrane as part of the pathway, is targeted by the phase-2 multidrug resistance modulator biricodar. MAP kinase-activated protein kinase 2 (MAPKAPK2, CHEMBL2208), which activates ALOX5 via phosphorylation, is targeted at phase 1 by AT-13148, a broad-spectrum kinase inhibitor. Three pathway enzymes — arachidonate 15-lipoxygenase (ALOX15), prostaglandin reductase 1 (PTGR1), and leukotriene C4 synthase (LTC4S) — lack ChEMBL mechanism records despite participating in the pathway, reflecting their lower prioritisation as drug targets in clinical development programmes.
baseline_answer: The human enzymes with catalytic roles in the leukotriene and eoxin biosynthesis pathway that are documented drug targets in ChEMBL with mechanism-of-action records include ALOX5 (arachidonate 5-lipoxygenase), which catalyzes the initial oxidation of arachidonic acid to 5-HPETE and its subsequent conversion to leukotriene A4, and is targeted by zileuton, a direct 5-lipoxygenase inhibitor approved for asthma treatment. LTA4H (leukotriene A4 hydrolase) converts leukotriene A4 to the pro-inflammatory mediator leukotriene B4 and has inhibitors documented in ChEMBL, though none are currently marketed drugs. LTC4S (leukotriene C4 synthase) conjugates leukotriene A4 with glutathione to form leukotriene C4, the precursor of cysteinyl leukotrienes, and while it has been investigated as a drug target with compounds in ChEMBL, no approved drugs directly inhibit this enzyme. ALOX15 (arachidonate 15-lipoxygenase), which is involved in eoxin biosynthesis by converting arachidonic acid to 15-HPETE and subsequently to eoxin precursors, has inhibitors documented in ChEMBL as part of research efforts, though none are approved therapeutics. Additionally, DPEP1 (dipeptidase 1) and GGT1 (gamma-glutamyltransferase 1), which sequentially remove amino acids from leukotriene C4 to generate leukotrienes D4 and E4, are present in ChEMBL as potential targets, though they are not primary therapeutic targets in this pathway.
togomcp_answer: Three human enzymes with catalytic roles in the leukotriene and eoxin biosynthesis pathway are documented drug targets in ChEMBL with mechanism-of-action records: polyunsaturated fatty acid 5-lipoxygenase (ALOX5, UniProt P09917, EC 1.13.11.34), which catalyzes the initial conversion of arachidonic acid to leukotriene A4 and has eight documented inhibitor mechanisms including zileuton and atreleuton; leukotriene A-4 hydrolase (LTA4H, UniProt P09960, EC 3.3.2.6), which converts leukotriene A4 to leukotriene B4 and has one documented inhibitor mechanism with acebilustat; and cytosolic phospholipase A2 (PLA2G4A, UniProt P47712, EC 3.1.1.4), which liberates arachidonic acid from membrane phospholipids as the initial step in eicosanoid biosynthesis and has one documented inhibitor mechanism with giripladib. Two other catalytic enzymes in these pathways, 15-lipoxygenase (ALOX15, P16050) essential for eoxin biosynthesis and leukotriene C4 synthase (LTC4S, Q16873, EC 4.4.1.20) which produces leukotriene C4 and eoxin C4, are present in ChEMBL as targets but lack documented mechanism-of-action records.

question:
Which parent disease category of systemic lupus erythematosus has the most diseases with confirmed MeSH TopicalDescriptor mappings?

Choose one of the following options:
  - immune system disorder
  - autoimmune disease
  - connective tissue disorder
  - rheumatic disorder
ideal_answer: Immune system disorder (MONDO:0005046) has the most diseases with confirmed MeSH TopicalDescriptor mappings among the four parent categories of systemic lupus erythematosus, with 248 confirmed MeSH TopicalDescriptors across its disease subclasses. This substantially exceeds connective tissue disorder (134 TopicalDescriptors), autoimmune disease (61 TopicalDescriptors), and rheumatic disorder (39 TopicalDescriptors). The counts reflect confirmed joins with the MeSH RDF graph restricted to meshv:TopicalDescriptor entries (i.e., canonical MeSH headings used for indexing); MONDO cross-references to MeSH supplementary concept records (MESH:C...) are excluded. The four categories are not mutually exclusive: immune system disorder is the broadest and contains the other three as subcategories, so their diseases are counted within it as well — the sum across categories (482) exceeds the 346 unique diseases with TopicalDescriptor mappings due to this intentional overlap in the ontology hierarchy. The higher absolute count for immune system disorder reflects its greater scope rather than a higher per-disease mapping rate, since autoimmune disease and rheumatic disorder actually have proportionally good coverage relative to their size.
baseline_answer:
The correct answer is **autoimmune disease**.

Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is classified under multiple parent disease categories in medical ontologies, including immune system disorders, autoimmune diseases, connective tissue disorders, and rheumatic disorders. Among these, autoimmune disease represents the most specific and well-defined category with the highest number of diseases that have confirmed MeSH (Medical Subject Headings) TopicalDescriptor mappings. This is because the autoimmune disease category encompasses a relatively focused group of conditions characterized by immune system attacks on self-tissues, and MeSH maintains comprehensive mappings for these well-characterized immunological disorders. While immune system disorders is a broader parent category that would theoretically include more diseases, the question specifically asks about parent categories of SLE, and autoimmune disease serves as the primary classification node with the most robust MeSH descriptor associations for related conditions. The other categories (connective tissue disorders and rheumatic disorders) are more heterogeneous and include many non-autoimmune conditions, resulting in fewer diseases with confirmed MeSH mappings within SLE's specific disease family.
togomcp_answer:
**Immune system disorder**

Immune system disorder (MONDO:0005046) has 249 diseases with confirmed MeSH TopicalDescriptor mappings, the highest among all four parent categories of systemic lupus erythematosus. Connective tissue disorder follows with 132 mappings, autoimmune disease with 59, and rheumatic disorder with 38. These counts represent diseases within each hierarchical category that have verified cross-references to MeSH TopicalDescriptor entities, excluding deprecated terms and non-TopicalDescriptor MeSH records.

question: How many distinct PubChem compounds participate in chlorophyll and bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis reactions documented in Rhea?
ideal_answer: Chlorophyll and bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis involves the enzymatic conversion of tetrapyrrole precursors into photosynthetically active pigments. The Rhea database documents these biochemical reactions by linking them to chemical entities in ChEBI. Querying Rhea reactions whose equation text mentions "chlorophyll" or "bacteriochlorophyll" and extracting their associated ChEBI compound identifiers (via the rh:chebi property) yields 64 unique ChEBI chemical entities involved in these pathways. These ChEBI classes serve as type annotations for PubChem compound entries (via rdf:type assertions), enabling cross-database integration. Filtering for PubChem compounds (those with URIs starting with "http://rdf.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubchem/compound/") that are typed by any of these 64 ChEBI classes yields 24 distinct compounds. These include central pathway intermediates such as protoporphyrinogen IX, Mg-protoporphyrin IX, and various chlorophyllide derivatives, as well as cofactors like NADPH, ATP, and S-adenosyl-L-methionine. The substantially smaller number of PubChem entries relative to ChEBI classes reflects the fact that many highly specific tetrapyrrole intermediates and bacteriochlorophyll precursors, though well-defined in ChEBI, do not yet have curated entries in PubChem.
baseline_answer: I cannot provide a specific numerical answer to this question as it requires real-time access to the Rhea database to query and count distinct PubChem compound identifiers associated with chlorophyll and bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis reactions. Rhea is a comprehensive expert-curated knowledgebase of biochemical reactions that integrates with multiple databases including PubChem for compound identifiers, but the exact number of distinct compounds participating in these specific biosynthetic pathways would need to be determined through direct database queries. The chlorophyll and bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis pathways share many common intermediates up to protoporphyrin IX, after which they diverge, involving numerous enzymatic steps with substrates, products, and cofactors that would each be represented by unique PubChem compound identifiers. Without access to current Rhea database contents, I cannot state with certainty how many distinct PubChem compounds are documented in these pathways.
togomcp_answer: Based on comprehensive querying of the Rhea database for chlorophyll and bacteriochlorophyll biosynthesis reactions and subsequent mapping to PubChem, **37 distinct PubChem compounds** participate in these biosynthesis pathways. The analysis identified 52 distinct ChEBI entities in Rhea reactions containing chlorophyll, chlorophyllide, bacteriochlorophyll, bacteriochlorophyllide, or protochlorophyllide terms while excluding degradation reactions (those producing catabolites, pheophytin, or pheophorbide). These ChEBI identifiers were converted to PubChem compound IDs through TogOID mapping (which yielded 20 matches) and manual verification via PubChem's compound name search (which found 17 additional compounds). The compounds include the core pigment molecules (chlorophylls a and b, bacteriochlorophyll a), biosynthetic intermediates (protochlorophyllides, chlorophyllides, bacteriochlorophyllides, Mg-protoporphyrin derivatives), precursor components (phytol, phytyl diphosphate), and essential cofactors (NAD+, NADH, NADP+, NADPH, ATP, S-adenosyl-L-methionine, water, protons, diphosphate). Several ChEBI entries representing generic compound classes rather than specific molecules, along with some highly specialized bacteriochlorophyllide variants, lacked corresponding PubChem entries and were appropriately excluded from the count.

question: Does human decorin (UniProt:P07585) have documented glycosylation sites in the GlyCosmos database?
ideal_answer: Yes, human decorin (UniProt:P07585) has documented glycosylation sites in the GlyCosmos database. Decorin is a small leucine-rich proteoglycan bearing glycosylation at six distinct amino acid positions: 34, 115, 211, 255, 262, and 303. The database records 286 total glycosylation site entries for decorin, reflecting the multiple glycan structures (glycoforms) annotated at individual positions — position 211 alone carries 132 distinct glycoform entries and position 262 carries 121, indicating extensive N-glycan microheterogeneity at these asparagine residues. The site at position 34 corresponds to the canonical glycosaminoglycan (chondroitin/dermatan sulfate) attachment serine characteristic of the proteoglycan core protein. This rich glycosylation complexity is consistent with decorin's biological roles in extracellular matrix organization, collagen fibrillogenesis, and TGF-β sequestration, where the glycan chains modulate protein–protein interactions and tissue hydration.

However, based on structural knowledge of decorin, this small leucine-rich proteoglycan contains a single glycosaminoglycan (GAG) attachment site near its N-terminus, specifically at serine-4 in the consensus sequence SGXG, where a chondroitin sulfate or dermatan sulfate chain is typically attached. Additionally, decorin contains N-linked glycosylation sites, with documented sites including asparagine residues at positions 211, 262, and 303 within its leucine-rich repeat domain. The protein's core polypeptide is approximately 40 kDa, but with glycosylation modifications, the mature proteoglycan reaches approximately 90-140 kDa depending on the size of the attached GAG chain. question: Which 5 genes have the most documented pathogenic or likely pathogenic ClinVar variants for Joubert syndrome, ranked by variant count?
ideal_answer: Integrating NANDO rare disease registry data, MedGen medical genetics concepts, and ClinVar variant data across all 51 Joubert syndrome MedGen concepts reveals 190 distinct genes with documented pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants. The five most heavily annotated are CEP290 (529 current P/LP variants), RPGRIP1L (255), CC2D2A (242), AHI1 (222), and TMEM67 (149). CEP290 encodes a centrosomal protein localised to primary cilia and is mutated across numerous ciliopathy subtypes including Joubert syndrome types 5 and 14, Bardet-Biedl syndrome, and nephronophthisis. RPGRIP1L (Joubert syndrome 7) and CC2D2A (Joubert syndrome 9) encode transition zone components critical for ciliary gate integrity, while AHI1 (jouberin, Joubert syndrome 3) mediates cerebellar and cortical development. TMEM67 (meckelin, Joubert syndrome 6) is a receptor-like transmembrane protein required for primary cilium formation. The high allelic burden in these genes reflects their role as major disease loci across multiple Joubert syndrome subtypes, the diagnostic importance of comprehensive multi-subtype variant cataloguing, and the evolutionary conservation of ciliary transition zone architecture.
baseline_answer: Based on ClinVar data, the five genes with the most documented pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants for Joubert syndrome are CEP290, which has the highest number of disease-causing variants due to its large size and critical role in ciliary function, followed by AHI1, TMEM67, CC2D2A, and RPGRIP1L. CEP290 typically contains several hundred pathogenic variants, reflecting its status as one of the most commonly mutated genes in Joubert syndrome and related ciliopathies. AHI1 and TMEM67 each harbor dozens to over a hundred pathogenic variants, as these genes are frequently implicated in Joubert syndrome cases and encode proteins essential for ciliary structure and signaling. CC2D2A and RPGRIP1L similarly contain substantial numbers of pathogenic variants, with each gene having tens to over a hundred documented disease-causing mutations in ClinVar. These five genes collectively account for a significant proportion of genetically confirmed Joubert syndrome cases, though the exact variant counts fluctuate as ClinVar is continuously updated with new clinical interpretations and submissions from diagnostic laboratories worldwide.

These counts encompass variants across all Joubert syndrome subtypes and related disorders documented in ClinVar's database, reflecting the genetic heterogeneity of this ciliopathy disorder characterized by the distinctive molar tooth sign on brain imaging and associated neurological and developmental abnormalities.

question: How many reviewed UniProt proteins are annotated with the GO molecular function 'hormone activity' or any of its descendant terms?
ideal_answer: There are 2,453 reviewed proteins annotated with hormone activity across all organisms in UniProt. This count encompasses proteins with the GO molecular function 'hormone activity' (GO:0005179) and all 19 descendant terms representing specific hormone types, including growth hormone activity, digestive hormone activity, neuropeptide hormone activity, and insect-specific hormone activities such as prothoracicotrophic hormone and ecdysis-triggering hormone. The taxonomic distribution reveals that hormone proteins are predominantly found in mammals, with Mus musculus (138 proteins), Homo sapiens (134 proteins), and Rattus norvegicus (123 proteins) representing the top three species. Significant representation is also observed in other vertebrates (83 proteins in Bos taurus, 67 in Sus scrofa), insects (39 in Drosophila melanogaster, 37 in Bombyx mori), and notably in the plant Arabidopsis thaliana (57 proteins), reflecting the evolutionary conservation and diversification of hormonal signaling systems across eukaryotes.

question: Characterize the reviewed late proteins of Enterobacteria phage T4 across three dimensions: their GO-annotated functional module assignments (capsid, baseplate, and tail components); any enzymatic activities encoded among these otherwise structural late proteins; and their experimental structural coverage, including the relative proportions of cryo-electron microscopy versus X-ray crystallography depositions.
ideal_answer: The 25 reviewed late proteins of Enterobacteria phage T4, all annotated with KW-0426, collectively encode the phage's iconic contractile tail-injection nanomachine and partition into four major GO-defined structural modules: the capsid (2 proteins: CAPSH/major capsid protein, CAPSP/capsid vertex), the baseplate (11 proteins: the ten wedge and hub assembly proteins gp7, gp10, gp25, gp27, gp48, gp53, gp54, gp26, gp28, gp51, plus the pre-baseplate central spike gp5), the tail tube and completion system (4 proteins: gp19 tube, tail tube terminator, tape-measure protein, gp15 tail completion protein), and the tail fibers (5 proteins: short fiber gp12, three long-fiber subunits gp35/gp36/gp37, and the long-fiber proximal subunit), with the structural collar protein fibritin (WAC) and two DNA helicase isoforms (uvsW and uvsW1) completing the set. Enzymatically, two late proteins break the "purely structural" expectation: gp5 (NEEDL_BPT4) bears lysozyme activity (GO:0003796) that hydrolyzes peptidoglycan in the bacterial cell wall to puncture the host envelope during injection, while the uvsW DNA helicases (GO:0043138, 3'-5' DNA helicase activity) drive late-phase recombination and repair, a function unusual for proteins expressed after DNA replication has begun. Of the 25 proteins, 17 (68%) are structurally characterized in PDB via at least one entry, yielding 149 distinct structures (103 by cryo-electron microscopy, 44 by X-ray crystallography, 2 by solution NMR); because T4 assemblies are large multi-subunit complexes—the baseplate alone contains 11 late proteins—the same PDB entry frequently references multiple T4 proteins, producing 182 total protein-to-entry attributions for 149 distinct structures (33 attribution counts arise from co-occurrence in assemblies). The striking dominance of cryo-EM (69% of entries) reflects the field's shift toward studying intact viral assemblies: fibritin alone accounts for 104 of these entries due to its adoption as an engineering scaffold, while the gp5 spike (18 entries) and major capsid protein (9 entries) follow; the eight structurally uncharacterized proteins—most notably the three baseplate hub proteins gp26, gp28, and gp51, the two long tail-fiber subunits gp35 and gp36, and the two uvsW helicase isoforms—represent current frontiers in T4 structural biology.
baseline_answer: The late structural proteins of bacteriophage T4 are organized into three primary functional modules: the icosahedral capsid (including major capsid protein gp23, vertex proteins gp24 and gp20, and portal protein gp20), the baseplate complex (comprising ~15 proteins including gp5, gp6, gp7, gp8, gp9, gp10, gp11, gp25, gp27, gp48, and gp53), and the contractile tail apparatus (featuring tail sheath protein gp18, tail tube protein gp19, tail terminator proteins, and tail fiber proteins gp34, gp35, gp36, and gp37). Despite their primarily structural roles, several late proteins possess enzymatic activities: gp5 forms the tail spike with endorhamnosidase activity for O-antigen cleavage; gp12 exhibits ATPase activity as the short tail fiber protein; whisker protein gp13.5 has sialidase activity; and the baseplate wedge protein gp5 also functions as a polysaccharide lyase. Structural characterization has been extensive but methodologically skewed, with cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) predominating for the assembled virion and large complexes like the baseplate (achieving resolutions of 3.3-7 Å for complete structures), accounting for approximately 60-70% of structural depositions in recent years, while X-ray crystallography has primarily resolved individual protein domains and smaller subassemblies (such as gp5 tail spike at 2.0-3.0 Å resolution, gp12 structures, and various baseplate protein domains), contributing the remaining 30-40% of structures and providing atomic-level detail for enzymatic mechanisms and protein-protein interfaces.
togomcp_answer: The reviewed late proteins of Enterobacteria phage T4 comprise 25 structural components distributed across three GO-annotated functional modules: three capsid proteins (major capsid protein gp23 forming the T=13 icosahedral shell, capsid vertex protein gp24, and the outer decoration protein Hoc), thirteen baseplate proteins (including gp5, gp5.4, gp6, gp7, gp8, gp9, gp10, gp11, gp25, gp27, gp48, gp53, and gp54 annotated to GO:0098025 virus tail baseplate), and nine tail components (tail sheath gp18, tail tube gp19, tail tube terminator gp3, short tail fiber gp12, long tail fibers gp35 and gp37, tail completion protein gp15, fibritin collar protein, and tail fiber assembly helper gp57). Notably, two baseplate proteins encode enzymatic activities despite their structural roles: gp5 possesses lysozyme activity (EC 3.2.1.17) for peptidoglycan hydrolysis during host cell wall penetration, and gp5.4 exhibits peptidoglycan beta-N-acetylmuramidase activity (GO:0033922), both functioning as molecular machinery for bacterial envelope disruption during infection. Experimental structural coverage is extensive, with twenty-two of the twenty-five late proteins (88%) represented by at least one structure among 161 total Protein Data Bank depositions, and the methodological distribution reveals a pronounced dominance of cryo-electron microscopy with 106 structures (65.8%) compared to fifty-three X-ray crystallography depositions (32.9%) and two NMR structures (1.2%), reflecting the field's shift toward cryo-EM for resolving large macromolecular assemblies and complete virion architectures that exceed the practical size limits of crystallographic approaches.

question:
Among the bacterial orders Hyphomicrobiales, Burkholderiales, Eubacteriales, and Pseudomonadales, which has the highest number of nitrogenase structural genes (nifH or nifD) represented in the NCBI Gene database?

Choose one of the following options:
  - Hyphomicrobiales
  - Burkholderiales
  - Eubacteriales
  - Pseudomonadales
ideal_answer: Hyphomicrobiales has the highest number of nitrogenase structural genes (nifH and nifD) among the four orders, with 66 annotated genes across 27 sequenced organisms in the NCBI Gene database. This order, which includes well-studied nitrogen-fixing alpha-proteobacteria such as Rhizobium phaseoli (6 genes), Rhodopseudomonas palustris (4 genes), and Xanthobacter autotrophicus (4 genes), is the predominant source of characterized nitrogenase genes in current genomic databases, reflecting decades of genome sequencing focused on agriculturally important symbiotic and free-living diazotrophs. Eubacteriales (formerly Clostridiales) ranks second with 19 nifH/nifD genes from 8 organisms, including Clostridium pasteurianum DSM 525 (7 genes) as the dominant contributor, alongside other anaerobic nitrogen fixers such as Clostridium tyrobutyricum (3 genes) and Clostridium beijerinckii (2 genes). Burkholderiales has 11 genes from 5 organisms including the endophyte Herbaspirillum seropedicae, while Pseudomonadales has only 7 genes from 2 organisms, notably Azotobacter chroococcum. The dominance of Hyphomicrobiales (66 genes, 64% of the 103 genes in these four orders) reflects the extensive genome sequencing of alpha-proteobacterial diazotrophs and the multiple nifH/nifD paralogs retained in some of these organisms. Across the full NCBI Gene database, 375 nifH/nifD genes span 174 organisms, demonstrating that nitrogen fixation capability is phylogenetically scattered well beyond these four orders, including in diverse Archaea (e.g., Methanocaldococcus) and sulfate-reducing bacteria.
baseline_answer:
The correct answer is **Hyphomicrobiales**.

Additionally, Hyphomicrobiales includes other diazotrophic genera like Methylobacterium and various photosynthetic bacteria. While Burkholderiales also contains notable nitrogen-fixing bacteria (such as Burkholderia and Paraburkholderia species, including some plant symbionts), and some Pseudomonadales members can fix nitrogen, the sheer diversity and abundance of well-characterized nitrogen-fixing lineages within Hyphomicrobiales, particularly the agriculturally important rhizobia that have been subject to extensive genomic sequencing, results in this order having the greatest representation of nif genes in genomic databases. Eubacteriales, being primarily composed of firmicutes including many pathogens and commensals, has relatively fewer nitrogen-fixing representatives.

question: Does Anabaena sp. DSM 101043 grow in nitrogen-free BG11- medium?
ideal_answer: Yes, Anabaena sp. DSM 101043 (BacDive ID 131407) is documented to grow in Cyanobacteria Medium BG11-, which is a nitrogen-free medium used to induce heterocyst formation in nitrogen-fixing cyanobacteria. A MediaDive query confirmed zero nitrogen-containing ingredients (no nitrate, nitrite, ammonium, or urea) in BG11-, establishing that the strain grows without any external fixed-nitrogen source. BG11- medium lacks fixed nitrogen sources, requiring nitrogen-fixing strains like Anabaena to form specialized heterocyst cells for atmospheric nitrogen fixation. This psychrophilic strain isolated from sediment can fix atmospheric nitrogen through the nitrogenase enzyme complex housed within heterocysts, specialized anaerobic cells that protect oxygen-sensitive nitrogenase from photosynthetic oxygen production. The cross-database integration between BacDive (strain characterization) and MediaDive (validated cultivation protocols) confirms this nitrogen-independent growth capability.
baseline_answer: Anabaena sp. DSM 101043, like other members of the genus Anabaena, is a filamentous cyanobacterium capable of nitrogen fixation through the formation of specialized cells called heterocysts. This strain can grow in nitrogen-free BG11- medium (also known as BG110 medium), which is the standard BG11 medium formulation without combined nitrogen sources such as sodium nitrate. When cultured in nitrogen-depleted conditions, Anabaena sp. DSM 101043 differentiates heterocysts along its filaments, which are the sites where the oxygen-sensitive enzyme nitrogenase converts atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into ammonia that can be assimilated by the organism. This ability to fix atmospheric nitrogen enables the strain to sustain growth in the absence of exogenous combined nitrogen sources, making BG11- medium a suitable cultivation medium for studying nitrogen fixation and heterocyst development in this cyanobacterial strain.
togomcp_answer: Yes. Anabaena sp. DSM 101043 grows in nitrogen-free BG11- medium (DSMZ Medium 1592) at 18°C. The medium lacks sodium nitrate, the primary nitrogen source in standard BG11, and contains only trace nitrogen from ferric ammonium citrate (0.006 g/L) used as an iron supplement. This growth pattern indicates the strain possesses nitrogen fixation capability, consistent with other Anabaena species that are well-characterized diazotrophs capable of converting atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia through specialized cells called heterocysts.

question: List all AMR element symbols associated with mupirocin resistance (MUPIROCIN class) in Staphylococcus aureus (NCBI taxon ID 1280) according to genomic resistance surveillance records, along with the count of isolate records for each element.
ideal_answer: Three AMR element symbols conferring mupirocin resistance are annotated in Staphylococcus aureus (NCBI taxon 1280) genotype records: mupA with 330 records, ileS_V588F with 90 records, and ileS_V631F with 5 records (total 425 genotype features; arithmetic check: 330+90+5=425). mupA encodes an acquired isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase paralog and is the primary determinant of high-level mupirocin resistance (MIC ≥512 mg/L), enabling competitive exclusion of mupirocin from the active site. ileS_V588F and ileS_V631F are point mutations in the native chromosomal ileS gene encoding the endogenous isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase and confer low-level resistance (MIC 8–256 mg/L). The predominance of mupA in surveillance records reflects its plasmid-mediated transmissibility, which facilitates its spread among clinical MRSA lineages, whereas ileS mutations arise independently and are less common. The distinction between high-level (mupA) and low-level (ileS mutations) resistance has direct clinical implications, as high-level resistance compromises mupirocin use for MRSA decolonization regimens.
baseline_answer: Based on genomic resistance surveillance data for Staphylococcus aureus (NCBI taxon 1280), the AMR element symbols associated with mupirocin resistance include mupA (also known as ileS-2), which encodes an isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase that confers high-level mupirocin resistance and is typically found on plasmids, and mupB (ileS-3), which provides low-level mupirocin resistance. Additionally, chromosomal point mutations in the native ileS gene encoding isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase can confer mupirocin resistance. In surveillance databases, mupA is the most frequently detected element, with thousands of isolate records documenting its presence across diverse geographic regions and healthcare settings, while mupB is less commonly reported with hundreds of isolate records. Specific numerical counts vary by database and time period of data collection, but mupA consistently represents the predominant mechanism of mupirocin resistance in S. aureus clinical isolates, particularly in methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) strains where mupirocin is used for decolonization. The ileS chromosomal mutations are documented in fewer isolate records compared to the acquired mupA gene, reflecting their relative rarity as a resistance mechanism in clinical surveillance.

The mupA gene represents the most frequently detected mupirocin resistance element, accounting for approximately 70% of the genotypic resistance features, while the two ileS variants (V588F and V631F mutations in the isoleucyl-tRNA synthetase gene) account for the remaining resistance determinants across 310 total S. aureus isolates in the surveillance database.

The sum of per-type counts (17,112) exceeds the count of unique articles across all five types (13,644), indicating that approximately 3,468 articles discuss multiple MPS types simultaneously. The disproportionate annotation volume for MPS III likely reflects its four biochemically distinct subtypes (A–D, caused by SGSH, NAGLU, HGSNAT, and GNS respectively) and its complex neurological pathology—MPS III is the most prevalent lysosomal storage disorder affecting the central nervous system and has attracted substantial genetics, biomarker, and gene therapy research. question: Summarize the human proteasome protein landscape as defined by the UniProt keyword KW-0647 (Proteasome), characterizing the distribution across GO cellular component subcomplexes (20S core, 19S regulatory, accessory), the overlap in multi-subcomplex annotations, and the fraction represented as ChEMBL drug targets.
ideal_answer: There are 53 reviewed human proteins classified under UniProt keyword KW-0647 (Proteasome), forming a structurally and functionally diverse complex system. When distributed across eight GO cellular component subcategories using structured ontology IRIs, 49 of these proteins are annotated to the general proteasome complex (GO:0000502), while the 20S core complex (GO:0005839) accounts for 18 proteins, the accessory complex (GO:0022624) for 16, the core beta-subunit complex (GO:0019774) for 11, the regulatory particle base subcomplex (GO:0008540) for 9, the lid subcomplex (GO:0008541) for 8, the alpha-subunit complex (GO:0019773) for 8, and the 19S regulatory particle (GO:0005838) for 7. The sum of these category counts reaches 126 annotations for 52 uniquely annotated proteins (arithmetic verification: 126 > 52 = valid overlap), reflecting extensive multi-subcomplex co-annotation — for example, a beta subunit is simultaneously annotated to the general proteasome complex, the 20S core, and the beta-subunit complex. Four proteins lack the general GO:0000502 annotation entirely: PSMF1 (PI31 inhibitor), PSME4 (PA200 activator), PSB11 (inducible beta-11), and PSMA8 (testis-specific alpha-8). Three of them (PSMF1, PSB11, PSMA8) still appear in subcomplex categories (GO:0005839, GO:0019774, GO:0019773 respectively), while PSME4 carries none of the eight GO subcomplex annotations, consistent with its role as an externally docking activator rather than a structural subunit. Cross-referencing with ChEMBL reveals that 22 of the 53 proteins (41.5%) are registered as human single-protein drug targets, with all eight canonical beta subunits included — consistent with the clinical use of proteasome inhibitors such as bortezomib and carfilzomib that target the beta5 catalytic subunit (PSMB5).
baseline_answer: The human proteasome protein landscape defined by UniProt keyword KW-0647 comprises approximately 45-50 proteins that partition into distinct functional subcomplexes based on GO cellular component annotations. The 20S core particle contains 28 subunits organized as seven α-subunits (PSMA1-7) and seven β-subunits (PSMB1-7, plus immunoproteasome variants PSMB8-10), forming the catalytic chamber responsible for proteolysis. The 19S regulatory particle consists of approximately 19 subunits divided into base (six ATPases PSMC1-6 plus PSMD1, PSMD2, PSMD4) and lid (PSMD3, PSMD6-14) subcomplexes that recognize, unfold, and translocate ubiquitinated substrates. Accessory proteins include alternative regulatory caps (PA28α/PSME1, PA28β/PSME2, PA28γ/PSME3, PA200/PSME4), assembly chaperones (PSMG1-4, POMP), and proteasome-interacting proteins. Multi-subcomplex annotation overlap is minimal since core and regulatory particles are structurally and functionally distinct, though some proteins like ADRM1/RPN13 bridge regulatory and substrate recognition functions. The fraction represented as ChEMBL drug targets is significant but limited, with the catalytic β-subunits, particularly PSMB5, being primary targets for approved drugs like bortezomib, carfilzomib, and ixazomib used in cancer therapy, representing approximately 15-20% of the total proteasome protein complement with documented small-molecule targeting and clinical relevance.

question: How many distinct human glycogenes in the GlyCosmos glycobiology database are annotated with the biological process 'protein N-linked glycosylation' (GO:0006487) or any of its descendant terms in the Gene Ontology?
ideal_answer:
Eighty-two distinct human glycogenes registered in GlyCosmos are annotated
with the GO biological process 'protein N-linked glycosylation' (GO:0006487)
or any of its seven descendant terms in the Gene Ontology (GO:0018279
protein N-linked glycosylation via asparagine; GO:0006491 N-glycan processing;
GO:1904381 Golgi apparatus mannose trimming; GO:0016256 N-glycan processing to
lysosome; GO:0016258 N-glycan diversification; GO:0180057 and GO:0180058 for
co- and post-translational variants). The per-term counts total 102 annotations
across the 82 unique genes, indicating that 20 genes carry two or more of these
GO terms simultaneously (for example, MAN1A2 is annotated with both GO:0006491
and GO:1904381). The set includes the dolichol-pathway transferases ALG1–ALG14
and the OST complex subunits STT3A/STT3B and DDOST/RPN1/RPN2, which catalyse
the core N-glycan assembly and transfer; mannose-trimming enzymes MAN1A1,
MAN1A2, MAN2A1, and MAN2A2; complex-type processing glycosyltransferases MGAT1–5;
glycan-remodelling enzymes UGGT1, UGGT2, and MOGS; as well as lysosomal
targeting enzymes GNPTAB and GNPTG. Many of these genes underlie congenital
disorders of glycosylation (CDG) when mutated, underscoring the clinical
relevance of this pathway.

question: Which manually reviewed human proteins annotated with the spinocerebellar ataxia keyword (KW-950) in UniProt lack a MANE Select transcript cross-reference in Ensembl?
ideal_answer: Among the 47 manually reviewed human proteins annotated with spinocerebellar ataxia, two—ataxin-2 (ATXN2, Q99700) and the spinocerebellar ataxia type 8 protein (ATXN8, Q156A1)—are not linked to a MANE Select transcript through their reviewed accessions in the Ensembl human genome annotation (Release 115). For the remaining 45 proteins, including ataxin-1 (ATXN1), ataxin-3 (ATXN3), CACNA1A, ITPR1, TBP, and PRKCG among others, their reviewed protein accessions are directly cross-referenced to MANE Select transcripts in Ensembl. The two exceptions arise from distinct annotation issues: for ATXN2, a MANE Select transcript (ENST00000673436) exists, but its encoded protein (ENSP00000500925) is cross-referenced only to an unreviewed TrEMBL accession (A0A5F9ZI57) rather than the canonical reviewed entry Q99700, reflecting an isoform-level discordance between the MANE Select transcript and the curated Swiss-Prot record; for ATXN8, the associated locus (ATXN8OS) is annotated as a non-coding RNA gene in Ensembl—its MANE Select transcript (ENST00000424524) lacks a translated protein with UniProt cross-reference, consistent with the fact that SCA8 pathogenesis involves a non-coding repeat expansion rather than a straightforward protein-coding gene.

question: Summarize the approved Rhea biochemical reactions catalyzed by the four gluconeogenesis-specific enzymes—pyruvate carboxylase (EC 6.4.1.1), GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (EC 4.1.1.32), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (EC 3.1.3.11), and glucose-6-phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.9)—covering the total number of approved Rhea reactions, the types of nucleotide cofactors involved (via ChEBI), and the number of reviewed human UniProt proteins annotated with each enzyme.
ideal_answer: Across the four gluconeogenesis-specific enzyme classes, Rhea contains 6 approved biochemical reactions in total: 1 for pyruvate carboxylase (EC 6.4.1.1, RHEA:20844), 2 for GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (EC 4.1.1.32, RHEA:10388 and RHEA:64864), 1 for fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (EC 3.1.3.11, RHEA:11064), and 2 for glucose-6-phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.9, RHEA:16689 and RHEA:44904). Regarding nucleotide cofactors, pyruvate carboxylase uses ATP (CHEBI:15422) to drive the carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate, while the human GTP-dependent PEPCK isoform uses GTP (CHEBI:37565) to decarboxylate and phosphorylate oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate; the phosphohydrolases—fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase and glucose-6-phosphatase—require no nucleotide cofactor, instead cleaving phosphate ester bonds using water. In reviewed human UniProt entries, there is 1 protein for pyruvate carboxylase (PYC_HUMAN, P11498), 2 for GTP-dependent PEPCK (cytosolic PCKGC_HUMAN P35558 and mitochondrial PCKGM_HUMAN Q16822), 2 for fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (liver F16P1_HUMAN P09467 and muscle F16P2_HUMAN O00757), and 3 for glucose-6-phosphatase (G6PC1_HUMAN P35575, G6PC2_HUMAN Q9NQR9, and G6PC3_HUMAN Q9BUM1), giving 8 reviewed human proteins in total. This pattern reflects tissue-specific isoform diversification in the regulatory phosphatase steps, in contrast to the single isoform covering the biotin-dependent carboxylation step at the pathway entry point.
baseline_answer: Pyruvate carboxylase (EC 6.4.1.1) catalyzes 4 approved Rhea reactions involving the ATP-dependent carboxylation of pyruvate to oxaloacetate, utilizing ATP as a nucleotide cofactor along with biotin as a prosthetic group, and is represented by 1 reviewed human UniProt protein (PC, P11498). GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (EC 4.1.1.32) catalyzes 2 approved Rhea reactions that decarboxylate and phosphorylate oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate using GTP as the nucleotide cofactor, with 2 reviewed human UniProt proteins (PCK1, P35558 and PCK2, Q16822) annotated with this activity. Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (EC 3.1.3.11) catalyzes 1 approved Rhea reaction hydrolyzing fructose-1,6-bisphosphate to fructose-6-phosphate and inorganic phosphate without requiring nucleotide cofactors, and is associated with 2 reviewed human UniProt proteins (FBP1, P09467 and FBP2, O00757). Glucose-6-phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.9) catalyzes 1 approved Rhea reaction that hydrolyzes glucose-6-phosphate to glucose and inorganic phosphate without nucleotide cofactor involvement, and is represented by 3 reviewed human UniProt proteins (G6PC1, P35575; G6PC2, Q9NQR9; and G6PC3, Q9BUM1).
togomcp_answer: The four gluconeogenesis-specific enzymes—pyruvate carboxylase (EC 6.4.1.1), GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase (EC 4.1.1.32), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (EC 3.1.3.11), and glucose-6-phosphatase (EC 3.1.3.9)—collectively catalyze six approved Rhea biochemical reactions. Pyruvate carboxylase catalyzes one reaction that converts pyruvate and bicarbonate to oxaloacetate using ATP as a cofactor, producing ADP and phosphate. GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase catalyzes two approved reactions: the primary reaction converting oxaloacetate and GTP to phosphoenolpyruvate, GDP, and CO₂, and a second reaction utilizing ITP in place of GTP to produce IDP. Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase and glucose-6-phosphatase each catalyze hydrolytic reactions (one and two reactions, respectively) that do not require nucleotide cofactors, using only water to produce phosphate and their respective sugar products. The nucleotide cofactors involved span three chemical families: adenosine nucleotides (ATP⁴⁻ and ADP³⁻), guanosine nucleotides (GTP⁴⁻ and GDP³⁻), and inosine nucleotides (ITP⁴⁻ and IDP³⁻), comprising both nucleoside triphosphates and their corresponding diphosphate products. Eight reviewed human UniProt proteins are annotated with these four enzyme classifications: one protein for pyruvate carboxylase, two proteins for GTP-dependent phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase, two proteins for fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and three proteins for glucose-6-phosphatase.

question:
Among the voltage-gated potassium channel genes KCNQ2, KCNQ3, KCNB1, and KCNT1 — each causally linked to distinct epilepsy syndromes classified under the epilepsy disease class (MONDO:0005027) — which gene has the greatest number of pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants currently documented in ClinVar?

Choose one of the following options:
  - KCNQ2
  - KCNQ3
  - KCNB1
  - KCNT1
ideal_answer: Among the four voltage-gated potassium channel genes KCNQ2, KCNQ3, KCNB1, and KCNT1 — all associated with MONDO-classified epilepsy syndromes — KCNQ2 (gene ID 3785, chromosome 20q13.33) has by far the greatest number of pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants in ClinVar, with 759 such variants, compared to 118 for KCNT1, and 102 each for KCNB1 and KCNQ3 (ClinVar January 2025 release; arithmetic verification: sum 1,081 > total distinct 1,077, indicating 4 variants attributed to multiple genes). This substantial variant burden reflects KCNQ2's role as the causal gene for both the severe DEE7 (developmental and epileptic encephalopathy 7, MONDO:0013387) and the milder benign familial neonatal convulsions type 1, making it a major target of clinical sequencing in pediatric epilepsy. KCNQ2 and KCNQ3 encode the Kv7.2 and Kv7.3 subunits that co-assemble to form the neuronal M-channel responsible for regulating neuronal excitability; KCNQ2 variants are more frequently pathogenic and more clinically diverse in severity than KCNQ3 variants, which mainly cause the milder BFNC2 (MONDO:0007366). KCNT1 encodes the sodium-activated KNa1.1 (SLACK) channel and causes the severe epilepsy of infancy with migrating focal seizures (EIMFS, MONDO:0100025), while KCNB1 encodes the Kv2.1 delayed-rectifier channel associated with DEE34 (MONDO:0014718); both have a smaller but clinically significant variant spectrum compared to KCNQ2.
baseline_answer:
**KCNQ2**

KCNQ2 has the greatest number of pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants documented in ClinVar among these four voltage-gated potassium channel genes. KCNQ2 is associated with a spectrum of epilepsy phenotypes ranging from benign familial neonatal epilepsy (BFNE) to severe developmental and epileptic encephalopathy, making it one of the most extensively studied epilepsy genes. The gene's clinical significance and relatively high mutation rate in epilepsy patients has led to comprehensive variant documentation, with several hundred pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants reported. In comparison, while KCNQ3, KCNB1, and KCNT1 are also important epilepsy genes with documented pathogenic variants, they have fewer reported variants in ClinVar, partly reflecting differences in disease prevalence, phenotypic severity, genetic testing rates, and the timeline of their discovery as epilepsy-associated genes. KCNQ2 was among the earliest epilepsy genes identified through positional cloning in the 1990s, contributing to its more extensive variant catalog.
togomcp_answer:
**KCNQ2**

KCNQ2 has 759 pathogenic or likely pathogenic germline variants documented in ClinVar, substantially exceeding the counts for the other three genes: KCNQ3 (102 variants), KCNB1 (102 variants), and KCNT1 (118 variants). This approximately 6-fold difference reflects KCNQ2's clinical prominence as one of the most frequently implicated genes in epileptic encephalopathies, particularly benign familial neonatal seizures and early infantile epileptic encephalopathy. The extensive variant catalog for KCNQ2 stems from both its high mutation rate and the intensive clinical sequencing efforts focused on this gene due to its well-established role in neonatal-onset seizure disorders.

question: Is 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine classified in PubChem as a pteridine (CHEBI:17601) and documented as a substrate in an approved Rhea reaction catalyzed by riboflavin synthase (EC 2.5.1.9)?
ideal_answer: Yes. 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine is classified in PubChem as the pteridine CHEBI:17601 (CID:168989), and its pH-7.3 microspecies (CHEBI:58201, CID:46931117) is documented as the direct substrate of the approved Rhea reaction RHEA:20772 catalyzed by riboflavin synthase (EC 2.5.1.9). In this reaction, two molecules of the lumazine intermediate undergo a dismutation: one is oxidized to release the four-carbon fragment that becomes the dimethylbenzene ring of riboflavin, while the other is reduced back to 5-amino-6-(D-ribitylamino)uracil, an earlier precursor in the riboflavin pathway. The reaction is chemically balanced, Approved in Rhea, and annotated with GO:0004746 (riboflavin synthase activity). The pteridine scaffold of the lumazine substrate is thus the direct biosynthetic precursor to the isoalloxazine ring system of riboflavin (vitamin B2).
baseline_answer: 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine is classified in PubChem as a pteridine derivative, as it contains the core pteridine ring structure (a bicyclic system comprising a pyrimidine ring fused to a pyrazine ring) that defines CHEBI:17601. This compound serves as a substrate in the Rhea-catalyzed reaction database for riboflavin synthase (EC 2.5.1.9), which catalyzes the final step of riboflavin (vitamin B2) biosynthesis. The enzymatic reaction involves the dismutation of two molecules of 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine to produce one molecule of riboflavin and one molecule of 5-amino-6-(1-D-ribitylamino)uracil. This reaction is well-documented in approved Rhea entries, as riboflavin synthase is a key enzyme in flavin biosynthesis found in bacteria, fungi, and plants. The classification as a pteridine is structurally accurate because lumazines are pteridine derivatives characterized by having a 2,4-dioxo substitution pattern on the pteridine core, and the presence of the ribityl side chain at position 8 does not alter the fundamental pteridine classification of the molecule.
togomcp_answer: Yes. 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine (PubChem CID168989) is classified in PubChem as a pteridine through its ChEBI ontology annotation CHEBI:17601. The compound is documented as a substrate in approved Rhea reaction RHEA:20772, which is catalyzed by riboflavin synthase (EC 2.5.1.9) and converts two molecules of 6,7-dimethyl-8-(1-D-ribityl)lumazine plus a proton into 5-amino-6-(D-ribitylamino)uracil and riboflavin.

question: How many distinct approved biochemical reactions in the Rhea database are collectively annotated for the reviewed human enzymatic catalysts of the nicotinate metabolism pathway?
ideal_answer: The 22 reviewed human enzymatic catalysts of the Reactome nicotinate metabolism pathway are collectively annotated with 58 distinct approved reactions in the Rhea database. This repertoire encompasses the full biochemical breadth of these enzymes beyond their nicotinate-specific roles: the pathway itself covers the Preiss-Handler route (nicotinate → NaMN → deamido-NAD+ → NAD+ via NAPRT, NMNAT1-3, and NADSYN1), the NAD+ salvage route (nicotinamide → NMN → NAD+ via NAMPT and NMNATs), and NAD+ consumption and recycling steps (CD38/BST1 glycohydrolases, NNMT methyltransferase, NT5E nucleotidase). The per-enzyme Rhea contribution varies widely: the sodium-coupled monocarboxylate transporter SLC5A8 (Q8N695) links to 14 approved reactions reflecting its broad substrate range for short-chain carboxylates including nicotinate, while NT5E/CD73 (P21589) contributes 10 reactions covering all its nucleoside monophosphate substrates. CD38 (P28907) accounts for 6 reactions including NAD+ glycohydrolase, ADP-ribosyl cyclase, and cyclic ADP-ribose hydrolase activities. The 58-reaction total from 22 enzymes (68 protein-reaction pairs) reflects 10 shared reactions catalysed by multiple enzyme paralogues — for instance, the core NAD+ glycohydrolase reaction (Rhea:16301) is shared by both CD38 and BST1/CD157. In contrast, NAMPT, NADK, NAPRT, QPRT, and NNMT each contribute a single Rhea reaction reflecting their narrow substrate specificity, while NMNAT1 (Q9HAN9) contributes 2 approved reactions — covering both NMN (Rhea:21360) and NaMN (Rhea:22860) as substrates — consistent with its dual role in both the salvage and Preiss-Handler de novo routes to NAD+.

question: Which reviewed UniProt Bacillus subtilis (strain 168) proteins annotated with the biotin biosynthesis keyword have at least one deposited structure in the Protein Data Bank?
ideal_answer: Three of the seven reviewed Bacillus subtilis (strain 168, taxon:224308) proteins annotated with the biotin biosynthesis keyword (KW-0093) have at least one structure deposited in the Protein Data Bank, while four — BioB, BioD, BioF2, and BioF1 (kbl) — are structurally uncharacterized. The three with PDB entries correspond to enzymes of the B. subtilis-specific pimelate thioester synthesis route that differs fundamentally from the E. coli BioC/BioH pathway: BioW (BIOW_BACSU, P53559), the 6-carboxyhexanoate–CoA ligase that activates pimelate as its CoA thioester, has four deposited structures (including PDB:5G1F and substrate-bound complexes); BioK (BIOK_BACSU, P53555), the L-Lysine–8-amino-7-oxononanoate transaminase, has four structures (including PDB:3DOD); and BioI (BIOI_BACSU, P53554), the cytochrome P450 that oxidatively cleaves long-chain acyl-ACPs to generate the pimeloyl-ACP precursor, has three structures (including PDB:3EJB). Notably, BioB (BIOB_BACSU, P53557) — the radical SAM enzyme that inserts the sulfur atoms to form the biotin dithiolane ring, constituting the committed final step of biotin biosynthesis — lacks any deposited structure in UniProt's PDB cross-references, illustrating that structural characterization does not correlate simply with biochemical importance.

P53555 (L-lysine--8-amino-7-oxononanoate transaminase, BioK) has four PDB structures (3DRD, 3DU4, 3DOD, 6WNN).
